segments.travel_time: 12-16 hours by express train (1880s); originally 24+ hours in 1869 on slower schedules
segments.cost: $20-$30 first class; $10-$15 emigrant class (1870s-1880s). Emigrant cars were converted boxcars with wooden benches, no upholstery, and a stove at one end for cooking.
segments.conditions: The line ran west from Omaha across the Nebraska prairie — flat, treeless, and monotonous for hundreds of miles. Passengers in emigrant cars sat on hard wooden benches or slept on straw-stuffed mattresses they brought themselves, jolted by the rough roadbed and choked by coal smoke pouring through open windows. At division points, the train stopped for 20 minutes at eating houses where passengers bolted down meals of tough beef, cold biscuits, and bad coffee. Blizzards in winter could halt trains for days on the open prairie, burying the tracks under drifts that required snowplow locomotives and section gangs with shovels.

West of Cheyenne, the railroad climbed Sherman Summit at 8,242 feet — the highest point on the transcontinental line — where passengers felt the thin air and saw snow on the peaks even in July. The track crossed the Red Desert of Wyoming, wound through Weber Canyon into Utah, and descended to the Great Salt Lake basin. At Promontory Summit on May 10, 1869, the Union Pacific met the Central Pacific and Leland Stanford drove the golden spike that joined the continent by rail. After 1869, passengers could travel from Omaha to Sacramento in four days — a journey that had taken wagon emigrants four to six months.

segments.from: Ogden, Utah (connection to Central Pacific / Southern Pacific)
segments.to: Sacramento and San Francisco, California (via Central Pacific / Southern Pacific)
segments.distance: approximately 690 miles from Ogden to San Francisco (via Sacramento)
segments.travel_time: 18-24 hours (1880s); total Omaha to San Francisco approximately 4 days
segments.cost: Through ticket Omaha to San Francisco: $65-$100 first class; $40 emigrant class (1880s). The Union Pacific sold land-grant parcels along its right-of-way at $2-$10/acre to settlers, often bundling land purchase with reduced-fare railroad tickets.
segments.conditions: At Ogden, passengers transferred to Central Pacific (later Southern Pacific) trains for the final leg across the Great Salt Lake desert and over the Sierra Nevada through tunnels blasted from granite by Chinese laborers in the 1860s. The descent from the summit into the Sacramento Valley revealed California spread below like a green quilt — orchards, wheat fields, and the promise that had pulled people across 1,776 miles of railroad and half a continent of prairie, desert, and mountain.

who_used_it: Homesteaders bound for Nebraska, Wyoming, and Utah claims under the Homestead Act of 1862. Gold and silver miners heading to Nevada, Colorado, and California. Mormon converts traveling to Salt Lake City. Chinese laborers who built the Central Pacific's half of the line (and were notably absent from the golden spike ceremony). Families from every state and a dozen European countries who rode emigrant cars west with their belongings stacked on the platform between cars. The Union Pacific was also a land-grant railroad, selling millions of acres of federal land along its right-of-way to settlers — making it simultaneously a transportation company and the largest real estate operation in the American West.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} boarded the Union Pacific at {origin}, riding the iron road west across the same prairie their parents might have crossed by wagon in six months — now compressed to four days of coal smoke, hard benches, and the rhythmic clatter of wheels carrying them toward {destination} and whatever the West had left to give.
source_references: Maury Klein, 'Union Pacific: The Birth of a Railroad, 1862-1893' (Doubleday, 1987)
Richard White, 'Railroaded: The Transcontinentals and the Making of Modern America' (W. W. Norton, 2011)
David Haward Bain, 'Empire Express: Building the First Transcontinental Railroad' (Viking Penguin, 1999)
Robert G. Athearn, 'Union Pacific Country' (Rand McNally, 1971)
